"There are faculties in the soul of man which correspond to the subtler planes of existence, which exert an influence on these planes, and are in turn influenced by them.  Most people ignore the existence of these planes and these faculties, yet if we observe life as it goes past us, we cannot fail to be struck by the fact that there are tides and eddies in the affairs of men which cannot be explained by the material sequence of cause and effect.  It is these subtle tides and eddies that the occultist studies, and he has been able as a result of his studies to formulate certain definite laws with regard to them." 
 
That's from Dion Fortune's Practical Occultism in Daily Life, one of the books of hers that did an enormous amount to shape my understanding of magic and the occult.   

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. comments.title: Death & Dying
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I’ve been doing a lot of exploring in terms of spiritual teachings, particularly after-death concepts. The teachings are all over the place, as you likely know. Most recently, I was listening to an audiobook about death and dying from a Buddhist perspective. I was not aware of just how harrowing the (Tibetan) Buddhist perspective on death was before now. According to these teachings, the last thought at death potentially determines everything else that comes after—various terrifying encounters with 52 wrathful deities and 48 peaceful ones (or something like that)—and then one’s reincarnation (where a person would likely end up as a donkey or other animal because their thoughts and karma were all over the place, with a human reincarnation—and thus, the prospect of awakening—very unlikely, etc.)

This struck me as fairly frightening—and then depressing. I suppose the universe doesn’t owe me or anyone else countless opportunities to clean themselves up and get with it, but the prospect of turning into a donkey and falling so far away from waking up is terrifying. I understand that Plotinus and other Westerners have posited similar after-death concepts, with the possibility of continual devolution to the point of becoming a plant. Then, on the other hand, there are the innumerable near-death experience and mediumship accounts of our current era in which experiencers return with a sense that Reality is much less exacting, much more forgiving and accepting of human foibles and weaknesses than more ancient concepts. Mediums tend to agree with this overall view, for whatever that’s worth.

What, in your view, is most essential to preparing oneself for a good death, good afterlife, and good rebirth? Which picture is more accurate—the Tibetan/Transmigration view or the contemporary NDE/mediumship view (or neither?). Any recommendations of previous articles, books, etc, are extremely welcome.

Thank you very much!

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
The religions of the Piscean era -- of which Mahayana Buddhism is one -- for some reason put a lot of time and energy into trying to scare people into obedience by threatening them with a horrible fate after death. That seems very counterproductive to me. The teachings that I follow derive partly from the Druid Revival tradition and partly from Western occultism, with its Pythagorean and Platonic roots; I draw on both because their accounts are clearly talking about the same thing, and each fills in blanks in the other.

First, the universe is not out to get you, and if the faith you follow leads to being assaulted by 52 wrathful deities when you die, you're probably doing something wrong. Your last thought means a lot less than the state of consciousness you're in when you die, and that's far less important than your overall state of consciousness in the last years of your life. When you die, if you practice religious devotion of any kind, you can expect the deity you worship, or an angel or emissary of that deity, to be there to help your soul through the process of shedding your material body and dealing with the transition -- that's one of the good things about religion. You may or may not be lucid during this process -- a lot of people go through it in something close to the dream state -- but it can be a very luminous and happy dream.

After a period of a few days, you pass through the second death (the shedding of the etheric body) and become conscious on the astral plane. Where on the astral plane you end up is determined by the state of consciousness you've reached in your mature years, and depending on that, it can be heaven or it can be hell. (In the occult view, this experience is where the idea of heaven and hell come from.) There are various stages in this part of the afterlife, but basically what you're doing is reliving the memories of the life just finished so that their lessons can be absorbed by your immortal soul. When that's finished, depending on how far you've developed on the mental plane, you may or may not waken to consciousness on the lowest level of the mental plane. If you do, you have some choice about some of the details of your next life.

Next comes rebirth. Your state of consciousness, the state of your karma, and the pressure of your destiny (the will of your higher self) all determine what this is. Under most circumstances, if you've reached the human level, you stay there until you're ready to go further. It can happen, if you mess up badly enough, that you can be reborn as an animal to absorb certain lessons you didn't develop the first time around; this is akin to being sent back a grade in school. Under most circumstances, though, you'll be reborn in a human body, and your parents, family, environment, and circumstances will be such as to assist you to confront your most important karmic issues and develop those human capacities you haven't yet developed.

As for what you can do, there are three things: ethical virtue, religious worship, and the practice of spiritual disciplines. Ethical virtue sees to it that you generate positive karma and help make sure the karma you face in your next life is easier than the one you had to deal with in this life. Religious worship sees to it that you have a connection with a deity who will help you in death as well as in life. Spiritual disciplines raise your level of consciousness so you can spend the time between lives in the heavenly rather than the hellish aspect of the astral plane, and it also brings you closer to the time when you can pass out of human incarnation and rise to what the old Druid teachings call Gwynfydd, the Luminous Life.

replies.content: A common magical discipline that can be described, in a certain sense, as controlled daydreaming. You enter into an imaginary landscape and let images and entities arise spontaneously in that context. There's a chapter about it in my book Paths of Wisdom, if you're interested.

replies.content: Is it like plotting a story?

replies.content: Try looking it up on your favorite search engine, then. I have more than a hundred other questions to answer, you know, and writing a long explanation of something you can find in any decent introductory book on ceremonial magic is not a particularly appealing use of my time.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: I did look it up.  Did you think your faithful Cutekitten would subject you to extra work?  But what I found says you need a teacher to do the pw with you, whereas I took your correspondent as saying he did it by himself, so I was confused.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
My faithful Cutekitten didn't identify herself until now, so for all I knew it was some wet-behind-the-ears goober who hadn't bothered to look it up. My apologies!

I've done pathworkings that were guided, as well as solo pathworkings, and much prefer the latter. The solo technique is simply to envision a doorway marked with some symbol on which you've meditated, then imagine yourself opening the doorway and stepping out into the landscape on the far side, which represents the symbol. You don't decide what the landscape will be, you let the imagery rise spontaneously, and you interact with it as though it was real -- walking through the landscape, talking with the beings you encounter, and so on. Then you return to the door, step back through, close it, and finish.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Not the OP, but the entire field of ethics is a mess right now, so what do you recommend for starting to figure out how to be ethical?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
Ignore everything written on the subject since 1900, and choose some code from before then that works for you. There's a very wide range of options -- the classical Pagan moralists are my cup of tea, for example, but other people may find something more to their taste in traditional Heathen morality, or one of the forms of Christian or Jewish moral thought, or what have you.

By and large, if some field is a mess today, assume it went down a blind alley and go back to the point at which things make sense for you, and you'll be fine. The past is a great resource.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
How can we prepare oneself to go through the process of death consciously?
From other traditions I have read that practicing going consciously into sleep prepares you a bit.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: I'd say don't worry about that. Going through it in the dream state is perfectly safe, and for many people, it's easier and less traumatic.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
Is that one of those things that come along the path if you are ready for it?

Also, the more I practice the more I find myself reliving some experiences and they seem to come in waves, for example a batch of memories from childhood or some unresolved things. Is this like doing that absorption while embodied or is that a different thing?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
Yes, the awakening comes when you're ripe for it.

That kind of spontaneous recollection is a very good sign; your astral body is clear enough that it's beginning to assemble its memories so they can be passed on to the higher levels of the self in good order. Old people who spend lots of time recalling the past are doing that, and it helps a great deal.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
Sweet!

It is as if life got kicked into into a higher gear. It is a very odd sensation because in a sense I always knew there was something in there but it was not clear what it was, it just played like a cassette. In general, rather than “seeing things” which is what I expected, it feels more like just been much more aware of what is happening within me. It is really weird to feel that there is so much in the in-between of two moments of awareness, to me it motivates me to be more present instead of wanting to go somewhere else. That is huge for me since I’ve been trying to runaway from my life for so long but now I know it doesn’t work because I would carry it with me no matter where I go... I don’t have to escape, I just have to pay attention!

One technical question though. Ever since I took this seriously life has just started to improve, I am not doing anything different in my daily life activities it is just that good things keep happening to me as if I got better “luck” What has changed?

Long ago I bought a notebook, way before I knew about this, it was a pleasant surprise that in the cover it has embedded an oak tree with some nice celtic knots adorning the edges. It came back to my attention when I finished the intro to CGD. It has become my study notebook. “The twine around the bulky envelope yields reluctantly to your shaking hands, but the glue on the envelope’s seal has long since turned to dust, and it opens readily. You pull out the papers inside, and read...”

Oh, and I will remember that about old people. Too many times I have seen them being called out for being “stuck in the past”

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: What has changed? Your level of consciousness. At the highest levels of human consciousness every action, however simple or humble, becomes a gesture of power and wisdom that brings blessings on everyone who encounters it. (Holy people routinely reach this state.) Even far, far below that level, as you raise your consciousness, yes, things just work better for you and everyone.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Why would being conscious be traumatic?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Most forms of dying are pretty unpleasant to go through. Do you really want to be fully conscious as you drown in your own lung fluids in end stage pneumonia, for example?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: So there's no way to be conscious for shedding the etheric plane but not for the moments of death on the physical plane?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: It sounds like you're trying to micromanage your own afterlife. Don't. The entities who will be helping you know what to do; you don't.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
Just a quick comment/clarification that the wrathful and peaceful deities in Tibetan Buddhism only appear to practitioners who have meditated on them in life. If you meditated on other deities you will see them instead.

Their purpose is not to terrify you but to remind you of the nature of your mind while you are going through the death process so that you can take advantage of your situation so as to not suffer involuntary rebirth. In the best case, you reach full Buddhahood during the death process.

Vajrayana says in general that people who were not practitioners will face a range of visions, stripping away of their consciousness of their previous life, before they reincarnate.

State of consciousness rather than final thought before death as you said too.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Thanks for this. My background in esoteric Buddhism is purely Japanese -- my stepfamily are Shingon Buddhists -- and that's technically Mantrayana rather than Vajrayana, so a lot of the details differ.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
Not the OP, but is there an option not to be reborn at all? Can you just kind of 'hang out' in limbo (for want of a better term) for a temporary or permanent period?

The next few hundred years seem like they're going to be fairly awful, if the fall of the Roman empire is any sort of model to go by, and if I have to come back at all, I'd rather do so once the dust has settled. Some might say that living through the suffering in such times would be good for karma, but I'd disagree if it means joining a roving warband to stay alive.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: You have to reach a fairly high level of spiritual development before you have any choice in the matter at all. One thing to keep in mind, though, is that collapse is geographically diverse; some places get hit much harder than others. Depending on your karma -- which depends in turn on how you spend this life -- you could end up in one of the regions that aren't affected so drastically.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: How can the afterlife be a version of Heaven or Hell, when the events of your life, which you review at this stage, already have happened and thus already have fixed, good or bad, outcomes? Or is there something else to it?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Partly it depends on your actions during this life -- you get to experience them objectively, without the filters of self-interest and egocentricity, so if you treated people badly you get to experience exactly what your behavior was like to the rest of the world. Partly, each level of the astral has its own emotional tone, and those on the lower astral are not pleasant.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
"Your overall state of consciousness in the last years of your life"

What if someone had dementia or a similar illness that seriously affected their mind for years before their death, but before that they had been quite spiritual and wise?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Have you spent time around people with dementia? What's affected by it is their capacity to think, not their character or underlying state of consciousness. When I worked in nursing homes I knew people with Alzheimers who radiated peace and happiness, and I knew others who were among the nastiest people I've ever met. That's the thing that determines destiny.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
Yes, although not all day several days a week as you would have working in a nursing home. Did you never meet one who was hostile and rude whose relatives said they used to be absolutely lovely and how difficult they found the personality change?

Though this isn't about one specific condition, rather about the general principle of illnesses which can cause drastic personality change and change behaviour towards other people. Some brain tumors would be another well-known example.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: There are usually karmic issues involved when that happens.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Does the aggression create more bad karma, although they can't help it? Do people/souls get stuck in loops of bad karma with things like this? Or would they work through it in the next life without the same necessarily happening again?

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Karma is the product of willed action. You don't create karma when you're not in control of yourself -- for example, if your unconscious body fell on someone and injured that person, you don't accrue karma from the injury. This is the same sort of thing. A demented person is not in control of his will, so doesn't create new karma.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
My grandfather has severe dementia. It's reached the point where he's unable to remember anything more than a minute on a good day, but from the above it sounds like he's going to be okay.

He's always offering to help with things; he still cracks jokes and if someone tells a joke he can follow he laughs; when I play music he recognizes he'll join in and start singing along. He has no idea what's going on, but he's fairly relaxed about it and in good spirits, and in general radiates peace and happiness.

1) What sort of afterlife experience will he have? He's never been a very religious person, so I don't think he'll have a deity help him (although I could be wrong).

2) While he's alive, are there any occult tricks I can use to help him and my grandmother? They live on the other side of the country so I only see them once or twice a year; I'm in regular contact by phone though.

I should also spectify I have a basic grasp of Qabalistic magic: of the sort you can get with a few months on it. Which I know isn't very much....

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content:
1) He sounds like a very pleasant person. He'll be fine -- he'll almost certainly go through the first and second deaths in a dream state and then become conscious again on a fairly high sub-plane of the astral.

2) Prayer is always an option; the name to invoke is ALHIM TzBAVTh, the name corresponding to Hod, which is the Sphere of healing. If you've learned how to do the Rose Cross Ritual, you can also do that, visualizing your grandparents in the middle of the space, receiving the blessing of the descending light; I've seen that do an enormous amount of good.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: I wonder how you know these things and how it is that you are so certain about them? Not just from reading, I’m sure.

replies.title: Re: Death & Dying
replies.content: Of course it's not just reading. It's 45 years of intensive spiritual practice, and the experiences and heightened perceptions that you can get yourself if you're willing to put in the work.

comments.title: “In Praise of Johnny Appleseed”
comments.content:
Hi John!
“In Praise of Johnny Appleseed” went a bit better this week. I still sat down sweaty, but… after last week’s Conan-pushing-the-millstone experience, I figured I’d call for more motive power. I looked at a map of the Ohio River, played “Mississippi Queen” by Mountain, and then recited the poem. Worked.
While catching my breath, I looked at my recently departed dog’s picture on my ancestor altar. One of his better tricks was going to a person by name. So… I said, “Go find Samuel Clemens. Ask for help.”. Among our national ancestors, I figured he would be among the most interested in averting a civil war… and probably favorable to the use of steamboats in magic.
Then, I read your post entitled “The Root of Madness,” and pondered how it is that a good chunk of the US population can’t hear itself. Today, I woke up thinking about “The War Prayer.” Hmm… “God's servant and yours has prayed his prayer. Has he paused and taken thought? Is it one prayer? No, it is two – one uttered, the other not.” Sounds right to me. I guess all I can do is take the advice to pay attention to what I’m thinking and saying.
https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_War_Prayer
Speaking of war – and please forgive me for being both new and dismal – what do Druids do when war (magical or temporal) becomes unavoidable?
Apologies for asking for a single source for the Celtic Pantheon last week. My background is in English lit, so I’m used to thinking of the Greek and Roman Pantheons as they appear in Renaissance and Enlightenment era arts. (You know – the ones that look like the bridge of the Enterprise.) Let me try again. The Druidry Handbook holy day ritual for Alban Elued invokes Esus. Is there anything short and online written by and for Druids that I can read for background?
Many thanks,
Rusty

replies.title: Re: “In Praise of Johnny Appleseed”
replies.content: It sounds to me as though Sam Clemens got the message and sent some help! It used to be quite traditional for the dead to talk to the living by way of thoughts suddenly surfacing on waking. As for war, as with most things about Druidry, one size emphatically does not fit all. Each Druid has to make his or her own decision in matters of conscience such as that. I don't know of anything short and online on Esus; you might just have to invite him and learn about him from the experience!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) Two weeks ago I asked about an apparent astral parasite, and think I have something else going on: I recognize it as provisional living. During one of my journaling sessions my subconscious went straight to "It's porn's fault". I asked how because it didn't make sense, and then it said "Porn is the root of all my problems."

I've then found my subconscious outright asking to talk about porn, since that would be easier. Each time I've defused it by asking if it'll actually be willing to address the porn problem, each time it says no and then I'm able to get it back on track by saying it has to pick one.

At this point my plan is still to address other problems first, since the issue is that I think porn has been so heavily weighted with emotional energies that I won't make much headway there for a while.

Is there a way to tell if it's just provisional living or there's an astral parasite involved? Either way, I plan to continue with journaling and working my way through LRM, and I'm far from sure it makes a difference...

2) One of the biggest issues I have is that I have what I'll be generous and call some entitlement issues. I feel I deserve a lot more than I have, and that I shouldn't need to work for it since the universe ought to just give me whatever I happen to want. I grew up very much upper middle class, and recognize this as a major issue with my class, and now I'm recognizing it in myself and I don't like the fact it shapes so much of my behaviour and attitudes towards the world.

Do you have advice on how to overcome this? I'm planning to continue to journal on the topic and try to address it and deflate my over developed ego, as well as making a habit of getting up to actually do things I need to do even if I don't want to do them, but is there anything else I can do to accelerate the process of dealing with this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Fascinating. No, there's no easy way to tell whether something like that is an astral parasite or an internal habit; over time, it may become clear, if (for example) the habit seems to have a very definite consciousness of its own.

2) You're fighting to overturn a lifetime of programming. Modern industrial society feeds the notion of entitlement to its inmates, since that makes them easy to manipulate. The crucial point is to be aware of it, recognize that it's cultural programming, and keep challenging it; that you can see it clearly shows that you've already won more than half the battle.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Well, so far no real sign of that.

2) Okay, so I'll plan to spend at least a couple years on this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have a suggestion for accelerating the process of dealing with habits of entitlement: volunteer. Preferably at a soup kitchen or homeless outreach, something of that sort. Volunteering with a crisis line might suit if you need something from home. Specifically, try to get for others whatever it is you think the universe ought to be giving to you. This is a tip given in Waldorf homeschooling circles for combating unbalanced tendencies of the melancholic temperament in children, namely feeling sorry for themselves. You handle it by strengthening the positive side of the melancholic temperament which is caring for others. It seems that this could still be helpful for adults.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is really interesting.  I'm not familiar with the Waldorf method at all , but acting in service to others has been enormously helpful in dealing with my own tendency toward feeling sorrybfor myself

replies.title: Animal Shelters
replies.content: I would second volunteering, though if dealing with people seems too challenging at this moment, consider volunteering at an animal shelter or adoption group. You can work up to helping more from there. Definitely, giving back with no expectation of getting anything in return is helpful to your own sense of being.

I'd heard about this story a few weeks ago and almost mentioned it here, seems more apropos now however. This fellow was electrocuted and "died" for 11 minutes:


“All of a sudden, I just woke up in a place that seemingly I’d always been. It was black and it kind of seemed like space,” said Tapp.

“I wasn’t Adam, I wasn’t a paramedic, I wasn’t anything. It was just like this raw form of consciousness where I was just existing very happily and pleasantly.”

“It took me a couple months — I just felt so foreign in my body.”

“What I take away from it is this absolute, unequivocal feeling that death is supposed to happen. It might not be pleasant and we might not want it, but it is supposed to happen and it is very literally a necessary part of all life.”

comments.title: Discursive Meditation for Kids
comments.content:
Last week, you wrote, “Children used to be taught simple forms of discursive meditation as soon as they could talk clearly. There should be no trouble with it at all.”

Are you willing to expand on what these juvenile forms of discursive meditation might look like? Or do you know a source that describes them?

replies.title: Re: Discursive Meditation for Kids
replies.content: Sure. Children were taught to keep a notebook in which they wrote down passages in the books they read that struck them as important, or moving, or worth serious thought; those passages then became themes for meditation. Selected Bible verses were also common themes for discursive meditation.  Another common approach was to take a list of virtues, and devote (say) a month to each of them; the child would spend a little time each morning thinking about that month's virtue, and then would try to do every day at least one thing that expressed that virtue in everyday life. Ben Franklin talks about doing this in his autobiography, iirc.

replies.title: Re: Discursive Meditation for Kids
replies.content: Adults would ideally do a banishing ritual before meditating, but that's not safe for children, so what's the next best alternative?

replies.title: Re: Discursive Meditation for Kids
replies.content: Prayer. Regular prayer, including a prayer for protection, was standard, and seems to have worked well.

replies.title: Re: Discursive Meditation for Kids
replies.content:
I worked with a woman who did this out loud daily with her kids. She had the Virtue Project cards (extraordinarily expensive now to get outside the US wth shipping, so I'm looking for something else), and every night at dinner or breakfast, whenever they were all together and not running to soccer practice, they'd pick a virtue, discuss it, and then think about how they practice it through the day.

She died, very suddenly and rather young, a few years ago. Her oldest was 20 (youngest 18) and managed to give the most beautiful eulogy to a room packed standing room only with seemingly everyone she'd ever met, and the daily meditation was what he said he was most grateful to her for at that time, because it prepared him to be a good man now that he'd have to do it without her, and he knew he could.

comments.title: Mishaps, Merlin and Mushrooms
comments.content:
Salutations JMG! My recent reading through Mystery Teachings from the Living Earth and the recent Cosmic Doctrine have felt as if you or something is speaking to me, as if it's finally the right time for me to internalize the lessons needed for the upcoming stage in life. It's really quite something to feel, so thank you.

1) On a previous Magic Monday somebody mentioned getting physically injured and having bad luck after the invoking form of the LRP. I didn't get to reply in time, but I've noticed the exact same thing - breaking things on the jobsite, bad luck and misfortune cropping up. Not in an extreme way, but days where I miss my LRP practice seem less eventful than days where I open the morning with a LIRP (yes I always banish at the end of the day). I'm not going to let it stop me though, I'll try a hoodoo bath and get my friend to perforate just outside my etheric body with a sharp knife in case it's some sort of entity. Could this be a karmic effect?

2) I'm planning to begin the Mysteries of Merlin on the Winter Solstice, and I think I might have passed the Ovate grade by then (despite the current round of delays). Do the rituals build momentum by being performed at the Ovate grades first, or would it be equally effective to start the Merlin rituals in the Bardic grade?

3) I won't be able to apply this knowledge for quite a while, but I'm still curious: what do you know about the magical dimensions of mushrooms?
It strikes me as odd that there is a massive body of astrological lore about herbs and trees, yet there is an entire Kingdom of organisms that are overlooked. A kingdom of life that is closer to animals than plants (they exhale carbon dioxide!), builds huge mycelium networks that look and function suspiciously like the neural networks of the brain, is capable of terrestrial bioluminescence, contains species with an antibiotic capability thousands of times higher (when compounded) than most commercial drugs, plays a significant role in the pollination cycles of bees, can break down toxins and oil spills in the soil, and is responsible for giving us bread and beer! If there's no magical book on the powers of mushrooms, then I intend to write one someday in the far future!
That being said, some caution would seem to be prudent. Fungi literally feed on death (although some feed on captured nematodes, and others are mutualistic with tree roots) and are clearly a telluric current phenomenon, which has its own risks if mishandled. Their position as the garbage disposal of nature that turns everything dead into fertile soil is quite Saturnian, which is to be respected and not treated lightly. Furthermore their connection to faery lore would imply that a mushroom worker might inevitably bump shoulders with some of the most powerful terrestrial spirits on the planet, which ought to make the hair on the back of anybody's neck stand up.

Risks being considered, what magical and ritual precautions would you suggest for working with fungi (beyond the obvious toxicological safety standards)? And if I've missed any resources like your Natural Magic Encyclopedia for fungi, I'd appreciate a pointer in the right direction.

replies.title: Re: Mishaps, Merlin and Mushrooms
replies.content:
1) It may be ordinary karmic culmination -- the tendency of spiritual training to bring a bunch of unresolved karma into focus in a hurry. Certainly going ahead is the right thing to do.

2) You should certainly do the Ovate Circle version first. It definitely builds momentum.

3) There's very, very little on that subject, and for good reason. Fungi don't just feed on death, they are the primary vessels for death-energies in nature. Death is part of nature, and both necessary and good in its place, but messing with death-energies magically can involve some pretty spectacular risks. If you feel called to research that, by all means, but be careful.

replies.title: Re: Mishaps, Merlin and Mushrooms
replies.content:
3) Could you expand on that? How are Fungi "primary vessels" rather than organisms that channel different aspects or their respective current (like herbs)? And does that apply to mutualistic fungi such as the mycorrhizae that feed trees with nutrients from their mycelium networks, thus allowing the trees to thrive in poor soil and giving the tree more nutrients than its roots could take up alone, for which they take a "finder's fee" in processed carbohydrate sugars from the tree's roots? Mycorrhizae seem more Mercurial, like an internet network shuffling nutrients around the forest.

Would precautions like invoking solar current forces or solar deities before attempting any experiments or workings be wise? What sorts of ways does a mage protect themselves when working with death energies and death-adjacent forces?

replies.content: I'm going to suggest instead that until you can answer these questions on the basis of your own knowledge of occult philosophy, it may not be wise for you to plunge into a field of magic that is widely considered rather dangerous...

replies.title: Re: Mishaps, Merlin and Mushrooms
replies.content:
Understandable. I'm nowhere near ready to begin such a research project anyway, I was just curious. Mycology gets me all fired up and excited thanks to its incredible potential in pharmacology and bioremediation, and lately I've been going over Paul Stamets' materials again.

If anybody else wants to geek out over mycelium, I highly recommend books and lectures by Paul Stamets. He's doing some truly groundbreaking work in the mass cultivation and application of rare mushroom strains from the depths of the Pacific Northwest forests. His TED talk (back when those were actually good and not just advertisements for unnecessary tech startups) back in 2008 was what first made my eyes light up around the idea of mycelial networks: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_5gUSyKTCx4

replies.title: Re: Mishaps, Merlin and Mushrooms
replies.content:
I've always thought the correspondences between fungi and Jesus to be striking - the parallels between resurrection and new life after death, and the centrality of bread and wine in the Christian mythos (both in the Mass, where depending on your theology Christ is literally bread and wine, and in his ability to make wine out of water and multiply bread). There's something there about the role of the telluric current in Christianity that could definitely stand up to a year of good discursive meditation.

Of course, the Fungi is a big kingdom, and yeast (which most of the ancient world probably just understood as a miraculous "something" in the air that made food happen) is about as distant, evolutionary, from a Destroying Angel mushroom as a sheep is from an earthworm.

Thanks very much for your work. I am working through “Paths of Wisdom while also reading the “Weird of Hali” series and enjoying it immensely despite never having been a huge Lovecraft fan! A mixed bag of initial questions if I may...

1. Does the mage operate in what Henry Corbin called the “active imagination”? I am approaching 2 months of this and plan to add in Middle Pillar after this 2 month period. Would I be correct in thinking this is adequate daily practice for the next year or so?

However the gods of Japan have never spoken to me whilst I have had spiritual experiences praying to the god of the Bible and when I read the qu’ran. replies.title: Re: Questions from a rootless capitalist financier
replies.content:
1) The active imagination is one of the primary tools of the mage. If Corbin was an initiate of a magical lodge, he was likely forbidden by his oaths of initiation from talking about magic too openly -- that was quite common in his time.

2) That's a very good set of practices. The one thing I'd encourage you to add is some form of divination; Tarot might be a good choice, since you're doing Golden Dawn work.

3) The call to Japan may not have been spiritual in nature -- it may have been that you had karma to work out there, or that you needed something in Japanese culture. If the god that calls to you is the Abrahamic god, go with that.

4) Here again, if you're doing Golden Dawn work, do it all the way and learn Hebrew. You can always do something else later, when you've mastered the Golden Dawn system.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I'm starting a long-term magical project of personal transformation. The aim of the project is to become charismatic and seductive to members of the opposite sex (I'm a man). Currently I don’t fit this profile for what I assume are psychological reasons. I have a deeply ingrained feeling of inferiority, a sense of being unlovable, and an insecurity that I believe gets projected into my behavior and affects my relationships with women.

I'd like to get your feedback on how to turn this into a reality. Following your advice on magical ethics, I decided to focus on changing myself rather than overriding the will of anyone else.

In general, I see the work as consisting of two parts:

i. Removal of limiting unconscious beliefs: feelings of inferiority, unlovability, insecurity, lack of self-confidence, low self-esteem, and so on.

ii. Substitution with alternate beliefs: confidence, high self-esteem, love of life and women

I believe that I have to change myself on a deep level, not just on a superficial one. It can’t be a matter of changing external behavior alone, since behavior is usually a reflection of underlying beliefs in the unconscious. I see the unconscious mind as being central to this project, because it guides our behavior most of the time. I have to change the unconscious first, and the behavior will follow suit. (As an aside, lots of contemporary dating advice tells men to make superficial changes like adopting a certain persona or appearance – I consider this to be ineffective because people are excellent judges of fakeness). I want to become the kind of man that women legitimately love and want to be with, for myself, not because I’m putting on a façade.

Given the above, I have the following questions:

1. What should be the general direction of the project? Should I deal with each issue in turn (for example, start by addressing the feelings of inferiority, then the unlovability, and so on) or take some other approach?

2. What kind of magical techniques are suitable? For the record, my background is in the Golden Dawn tradition (I’m almost done with LRM), and I have some basic knowledge of natural magic. I’m also familiar with more conventional psychotherapies like CBT. My daily practice consists of the LBRP, MP, tarot divination and meditation , which I’ve done daily for about 8 months (pretty introductory stuff, I know). Do I have enough tools to get the job done?

3. I get the impression that this is the work of several years. Do you have any general advice for me (or anyone in the commentariat who might share my aim) before I get started with this project? Are there any blind spots I should pay attention to?

4. Are you familiar with anyone who's attempted something similar, and if so, can you share some of the lessons learned?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Good. Your attitude is the kind that leads to constructive results.

1) The approach I'd suggest is to go deep. All those issues are knotted together in the deep places of your mind, and probably linked to old karma and past life experiences. To overcome them you need to awaken to a higher level of consciousness than the one you currently are at. That requires a balanced program of spiritual development combined with specific magical work to unravel the issues. Don't worry about individual symptoms; work on transforming yourself by coming into contact with suitable magical energies, which can straighten out the mess.

2) The Golden Dawn tradition is extremely well suited to this sort of thing, but you're going to need to add to your current toolkit. I'm going to risk tooting my own horn a bit and suggest that once you've finished LRM you go on to Circles of Power, which has the additional rituals you need. Once you've mastered the basic ingredients of magical ritual, you can then pursue workings for spiritual development and combine that with the creation and consecration of a talisman of Venus, and quite probably other Venereal magical workings -- for example, evoking the intelligence of Venus and asking for that entity's advice and help would be smart, once you get to the point of being able to do that.

3) Before you start, think hard about what the endpoint of your work will be. Do you want to end up happily married to a woman who loves you? Or do you want to end up having lots of women interested in you for less permanent relationships? Those are not the same thing by a long shot, and you really do have to choose between them.

4) I don't happen to know anyone who's tackled that specific set of issues, but doing magical work to transform unhelpful aspects of one's personality is all but universal among serious Golden Dawn mages. My advice above is drawn from that experience, on my own part as well as that of others. The most important advice I can offer, again, is not to address symptoms. Call the appropriate magical energies into play and let them address the problem at the level of root causes; the symptoms will then dissolve of themselves.

comments.title: Saturnine Musings
comments.content:
Hello JMG, Thank you for your continuing generosity in hosting this space and fostering the fabulous and illumining discussion which ensues.
With the themes with which this week's MM has begun, it is obviously opportune for me to continue in kind.

I'm pondering planetary charity or perhaps less formally an offering for Saturn. Namely, to tend a local cemetery. Although the grass is mown by Council, many of the old graves are neglected. They are covered in leaves, branches and bark from the surrounding eucalypt trees which are resistant to decomposition. The acid they leach is destructive to the sandstone structures.

According to Cornelius Agrippa, "all stinking places, dark, underground, religious and mournful places, as churchyards, tombs, and houses not inhabited by men, and old, tottering, obscure, dreadful houses, and solitary dens, caves and pits, also fishponds, standing pools, fens and such like are appropriated to Saturn." (thank you C. Warnock of Renaissance Astrology)

Cemeteries and decay being germane to Saturn, I'm diffident as to whether He would appreciate attempts to counter entropy there. (It seems possible He would consider tidying the graves to constitute interference...) Is tending disintegrating graves likely antithetical to His pleasure in disintegration? My personal aesthetic sense would argue for the reverse: there is a nice resonance between this activity and the wabi aesthetic (pointing out the beauty in that which is worn out, breaking down) and the cemetery/fungus axis. It's not as though I'd be restoring graves which are tumbledown and out of kilter, merely removing the rubbish that detracts from their peculiar wabi kind of beauty.

Not that I find them spooky at all, but do cemeteries' inhabitants generally present any threats to a well-wishing visitor? I don't have any personal connection to those buried around here and I perform a daily LBRP... are there other things to be watchful of?
I am also fascinated by Fungi, in a less active way than boulderchum, which are obviously among Saturn's interests and active in these places.

Not specially wanting reassurance for the good sense of my plot(!), but insights nd comments on this theme from you and others would be welcome.

Scintillating Iridescent Elver

comments.title: Moving house

Hi John,

Firstly, I just wanted to say a big thank you for this forum, in which I've learned more about the world in the last 10 months or so than the 10 years beforehand, and it's like every little snippet of weird knowledge I had from beforehand has all fallen into place as part of a semi-coherent theory of magic, and I'm seeing the world afresh with new eyes.

We are planning to move house soon, and I would like to ensure that I get the new house as etherically and astrally clean as possible when we move in. replies.title: Re: Moving house
replies.content: If you've got carpets, mix a pound or so of salt with a tablespoon of hot red pepper and scatter it all through the carpeted space. Beyond that, get some frankincense and burn some in each room, then air the whole house thoroughly. You can repeat the salt trick regularly -- and of course a daily banishing ritual is the best thing you can do, for yourself and your home.

replies.title: Emotionally sensitive
replies.content:
I have this same problem. The biggest thing is learning to sort out what emotions are actually mine and which I’m absorbing from around me. It’s less exhausting and easier to let go of if I know it’s borrowed, somehow. Journaling and careful self-awareness was the first step. I also eventually moved to the country and suddenly realized how much emotional static I was actually processing all the time!! Regular banishing rituals like JMG suggests seems to help keep the gunk from sticking, more like I feel a wave of something hit me and then it slides off. Practice intentionally shielding if you are face to face with somebody spewing emotion - usually I imagine a smooth bubble around myself so that the wave can simply flow around me but I remain unaffected. Practice this image when you don’t need it so that you can think “Shields up!” and have it spring up in a moment when you do need it.

And try to remember that this can also be a gift. I can work a room like nobody’s business, and the energy of a good concert can leave me floating for days. Work with it, don’t try to shut it down. All the best!

Forseti said "On the matter of the oath. We release you from it on the grounds of incapacity. It was beyond your strength of mind and body, your might and main, and you are no longer bound by it."

A Pause. Then, "On the matter of oathbreaking." OMG. Here it comes. "We find you not guilty of the crime of oathbreaking by reason of incapacity. You lack the judgment to know what you can and cannot swear to perform."

Baphomet was one of the two powers worshiped by the Knights Templar in their later, Gnostic phase; the other is Sophia, the spirit of wisdom, and the two names convert into each other by way of the Cabalistic Atbash cipher (a traditional way of working with holy names in Hebrew). comments.title: The metaphysics of passing out
comments.content:
I'm generally on board with the idea of an afterlife, but there is one small thing that has always bothered me. Basically, the idea that consciousness lives on after a person dies seems to rely on the idea that a person can stay conscious independently of their brain. When the brain switches off, the person stays conscious on a different level.

Now, the only personal experience I have relating to this is this: Once I had a fever, and while trying to make myself some tea I passed out and fell to the floor twice. While I was only out for a few seconds each time (certainly less than a minute), I was completely "switched off" for that time. I had absolutely no perception, no passing of time, no sense of self. I only figured out that I must have fallen from the fact that I was on the floor and had some bumps on my head. It was very different from sleeping, and even from medical sedation, where I always have some sense of time passing or fading in and out of dreams.

I'm certainly not the first person this has happened to, and I've read reports from people describing similar experiences when on the edge of medical death, and even from certain types of sleep. So maybe you know an explanation for this that is compatible with occultism? How can consciousness just switch off when the brain is compromised, why doesn't it persist on other levels?

This is not supposed to be nitpicking, this has honestly been bothering me for a long time. Thank you for taking questions!

replies.title: Re: The metaphysics of passing out
replies.content: In occult philosophy consciousness can be focused or diffused. Waking consciousness is focused. Sleeping consciousness is somewhat diffused -- in dreams, it's about half-focused, while in deep dreamless sleep it's much more diffuse. In unconsciousness it's as diffuse as it can get; in particular, it's diffuse enough that it no longer records memories. That's what gives you the "switched off" feeling. I've had it myself, while under anesthesia when I had my tonsils out at age 7, and then on several later occasions when I dropped into really deep dreamless sleep and blinked awake many hours later with no sense of time having passed at all.

replies.title: Re: The metaphysics of passing out
replies.content:
(Not the OP)

Thanks for this; I've been meditating on the fact that I almost never dream (once every few years or so) and almost always have the very deep, dreamless, no-sense-of-time-passing sleep, and was even considering asking about the occult significance of this. (But, I opted against it as meditation is proving fruitful.) And yet a similar question comes up! How funny how often that seems to happen in this channel.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

My question deals with the overlap between the circles of magic and politics: how do you know when you've gone crazy?

I mention this for a couple reasons: First, your post on the Root of Madness made me do a double take to confirm that yes, police are attacking peaceful protesters, and not because I say they are, but because there's video evidence of it:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UQIOXuwh9vo

Now, it's easy to see only what you want to, but what's even more compelling is all the evidence of police attacking journalists:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bSXdYNbS9DQ

Am I supposed to believe that all journalists are violent rioters and arsonists?

I don't doubt that many protests have become violent, but when police are inciting violence, rather than encouraging peaceful protests, we have a problem. If the police can attack anyone based on the fact that there were riots in another city, then we've lost the right to assembly.

Couple this together with Trump's statements on the record of not trusting the result of a democratic election and calling for a delay:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuLNffP5-pg

And I'm worried this November could be the last legitimate election Americans see until the country splits up.

In fairness, corruption has always played a role in American politics: the DNC famously became the "Democrats for Nixon" after they failed to strong arm a pro-war candidate down the throats of their anti-war voting base. But there's a line past which a corrupt democracy is simply an autocracy--and Trump's current strategy, amidst sinking approval ratings, seems to be attacking the democratic process itself.

I actually don't blame the destitute working class of America for wanting revenge on the comfortable classes by hook by crook. Trump's done a lot to give a voice for their discontent, even if he's tried harder to scapegoat marginalized people than he has to push forward the tariffs and other economic protections that actually make a difference. By comparison, the Biden-Harris platform is one of--dare I say it?--suffocation, as much for discontents in its own party as for working republicans. But no good intention can excuse the methods Trump promotes.

This all leads me to conclude that either I'm crazy for seeing signs of jackbooted oppression, or you're crazy for denying them, or there's a third possibility I haven't considered.

I've had democrats try to argue that Bernie Sanders must be a Russian Spy, so I don't consider madness to be a partisan issue. Most us treat ideas that confirm our assumptions as warm goo, and anything that challenges our assumptions as cold prickles, and we seem less and less concerned about living in the same world as our neighbors--especially when we can find an online neighborhood to confirm nearly any belief.

Given such an environment, how do I even know if I'm crazy?

This leads to my second point: being experimental in magic, I've interacted with such a wide variety of beings that I've started seeing religions as different sets of training wheels for a world so abundant with living entities that we could never tell enough stories to identify all of them.

I get the feeling that interacting with spirits is more like fishing, you start with a story, a symbol, a bit of incense, a spark of imagination, or all of these, in the hopes of catching something with its own intelligence and gifts to share. Sometimes I feel latched on to a tiny goldfish, other times to a great whale, and some spirits seem to grow or change over time.

So the related question is, when practicing magick, how do you know when you've caught a really big fish and when (as in your example with Michael M. Hughes) you've simply convinced yourself of it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There are several effective ways to keep an eye on your sanity and make sure that you're not going crazy. One of the best is to learn the standard logical fallacies and watch your thinking to see if you fall into them. If you get two Youtube clips taken out of context that show the police initiating violence, and argue on that basis that all the violence going on in America right now must therefore be entirely the fault of the police, why, that's a common logical fallacy, one that most people can see through very clearly when they're in their right minds. If you catch yourself doing that, it's time to reassess your thinking processes.

Another very useful way to keep track of your thinking is to watch your overall mental state. If you spend a lot of time being angry, offended, and full of hate toward people you see as the enemy, that's not a good sign; if you catch yourself spending lots of time watching things online that have no other purpose than to feed your anger, hatred, and offendedness, that's an even worse sign; and if you find yourself going onto other people's internet spaces and trying to pick fights with people who disagree with you, why, in that case you really need to take it easy for a while and reestablish your mental equilibrium.

2) The proof of the pudding is in the eating, and the proof of magic is in its effectiveness. You see what effects you get, and judge what kind of a fish you've caught on that basis.

comments.title: Quantum computers and magic
comments.content:
Hello John,

The other day I was reading an item is this technology newsletter that I get at work about linking quantum computers and AI. I get that this is a short-to-medium term concern since the Long Descent/Limits to Growth scenario implies that these things will eventually become impractical or impossible, but in the meantime, the thought of our elite classes gaining even more ways to game the system in their favor is not a happy one.

I've long thought that quantum physics was telling us something very deep about the universe, as it is possible to do repeatable experiments with quantum phenomena that demonstrate that the presence of an observer has an effect on the outcome. This suggests that consciousness does indeed have subtle interactions with the physical world. From a magical POV, if one's consciousness exists primarily on the astral plane, but directs one's body on the physical plane, quantum effects have to be considered as a candidate for how that happens when viewed from the physical plane. I am not trying to say that quantum physican and magic are the same thing, but more that they are different modles for parts of the same obliquely-glimpsed underlying reality.

So, connecting A and B: quantum computers should be uniquely vulnerable to magical interference. I did a divination, and the answer seems to be positive.

The actual question was "Are quantum events more susceptible to magical intervention than are classical physical events". I cast this as a geomantic house chart, assuming that the 8th house (magic by and for the Querent) was the proper house of the Quesited.

1 Via
2 Amissio
3 Via
4 Cauda Draconis
5 Via
6 Puella
7 Via
8 Amissio
9 Acquisitio
10 Tristitia
11 Rubeus
12 Acquisitio

If I'm reading this correctly, it perfects by double conjunction (Via to the 7th and Amissio to the 2nd), but also by translation with Acquisitio in the 9th and 12th. The one fly in the soup is Via square in the 5th. Beyond the yes, yes, yes though, there is a lot going on in this chart. The double conjunction would seem to imply that there is a very tight linkage between the question and answer, possibly to the extent of "quantum computers are technologically-mediated magic". As for the Acquisitio translation, I'm thinking the relevant correspondences of those houses are divination and secrets respectively. I'm not sure what to make of the square. Could you possibly add your expert interpretation of this chart?

However, if this line of thinking is too dangerous to be discussed publicly, please don't post it (in the spirit of keeping silent), though I would appreciate a brief reply that doesn't tread on any third rails.

Thank you.

PS. The last two tentacle fiction books are on deck after I finish Herotodus (which is a surprisingly good read).

replies.title: Re: Quantum computers and magic

That cat's been out of the bag for a long time. Parapsychologists determined back in the 1970s that quantum events are far more susceptible to precognitive and telekinetic psychism than macroscale events. Quantum computers will be profoundly vulnerable to manipulation both by human beings and by...other things. I don't imagine that will end well.

Your reading is yelling "YES!" at the top of its lungs; a square doesn't count for much of anything when weighed against three modes of perfection. The square simply indicates that the magical intervention may have unpredictable events -- remember that the 5th is the house of gambling!

comments.content:
Recently it hit me that spirits are very very old and intelligent beings that are fundamentaly strange to us and have their own interests. They are no friendly cartoon characters.
And the folklore concerning the good people in Ireland, the J_nn in the Middle East and so on reflects that. Goetic demons that were witnessed in babylonic times and the middle ages are still answering the rituals of the contemporary fools who call them.
The caution of organized religions concerning these things that I derided in the past makes much more sense to me now.

My question now is, how much of the magic in western tradition works via the cooperation of spirits?

I know that planetary spirits play an important role in geomancy. Your SoP calls for the blessings of gods and spirits. Are spirits a central working principle/ingredient in magic?

Also, as a polytheist, would you say there is a meaningful difference between Astaroth and Ishtar/Astarte? Is praying to the one the same as praying to the other?

Thank you, this is not my native language, so please excuse any grammatical oddities you might find.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends on what tradition of magic we're discussing. The Golden Dawn and its many variants, the kind I've mostly worked with, does not rely on spirits; there are rituals that you can use to interact with spirits if you want to, but most of the work does not -- it relies on the great impersonal currents of creative force that flow through the cosmos. Some of those can be personalized -- "every energy is an entity" -- but they don't have to be.

The name Astaroth is a Hebrew plural, and it adds to a different number than Astarte -- yes, that matters in magic. Astarte/Ishtar is a goddess, while Astaroth is a fragmented, distorted reflection of that same current of divine energy on a much lower and more unbalanced plane. That is to say, yes, there's a meaningful difference.

comments.title: Ancient Gods and Political Slogans
comments.content:
Although I've been reading here for a while, this is my first post, so I would like to thank you for hosting these discussions.

A while ago, there was a topic about how the motto "MAGA" was very magically resonant*, and "Keep America Great" was much less so. On a hunch I looked it up and, much like Kek, KAG is the start of the name of an Egyptian god, Kagemni. He was a vizier of the first pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty, and he was deified later. The Fourth Dynasty was ancient Egypt's golden age, which would be something that one would aspire to when launching a movement to revitalize the country.

I acknowledge that it's a pretty loose association, but I found it interesting. I don't really have a question, I just thought it's be relevant.

Wikipedia also has a Kagemni from the 6th Dynasty but he wasn't deified, and the deified one doesn't have a page, strangely enough.

*"Maga" was also used as the feminine form of "Magus" in the pen-and-paper RPG "Ars Magica".

replies.title: Re: Ancient Gods and Political Slogans
replies.content: Fascinating. No, I didn't know that! "Maga" is also the term used in Magus Mirabilis in Oz, the Latin version of The Wizard of Oz, for the witches of the four directions; Glinda, for example, was the Bona Maga Australis, the Good Maga of the South.

comments.title: Urim Thummim and Divination
comments.content:
From my Bible-studying days, I remember reading about the Urim and Thummim. These were lot-casting stones in the breastplate of the Israelite high priest, who would wear the breastplate while entering into the Holy of Holies on certain days in the Jewish calendar.
The Urim and Thummim were apparently removed and used to cast lots that would reveal directions from YHWH on what the Israelites were supposed to do (or not do) next.

Elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible texts, ordinary people were prohibited from any sort of divination, including casting lots. Putting these two together, it has me wondering if forbidding divination was a form of political control at that time, since getting a communication directly from God sort of bypasses the power structure.

To your knowledge, are there any occult commentaries on the Urim and Thummim?

Thanks again for these postings-- And get some rest, eh? Looks like you've been up for 24 hours straight...

--Emmanuel G
